content.content:
title:	Support %DEFINE value substitution in expressions (PL/I-style %DCL/%ACTIVATE)
state:	OPEN
author:	softwarewrighter (Software Wrighter LLC)
labels:	
comments:	1
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
number:	1
--
## Problem

`%DEFINE NAME VALUE;` currently only works for conditional compilation (`%IF DEFINED(NAME)`). It does **not** substitute `VALUE` when `NAME` appears in an expression.

```plsw
%DEFINE TAG_NULL 0;
X = TAG_NULL;    /* ERROR: undefined variable */
```

This forces using `DCL TAG_NULL INT STATIC INIT(0);` which allocates runtime storage for what should be a compile-time constant -- wasteful on a 24-bit embedded target with limited memory.

## How PL/I handles this

PL/I has a two-step mechanism:

```pli
%DCL BUFSIZE FIXED;
%BUFSIZE = 256;
%ACTIVATE BUFSIZE;
/* Now BUFSIZE is textually replaced with 256 everywhere */
Y = BUFSIZE + 1;   /* preprocessor outputs: Y = 256 + 1; */
```

- `%DCL` declares a preprocessor variable (CHARACTER or FIXED only)
- Assignment sets its value
- `%ACTIVATE` enables text substitution into the source

The variables vanish after preprocessing -- zero runtime cost.

## Proposed approach for PL/SW

A simpler single-step approach may be better than PL/I's two-step `%DCL`/`%ACTIVATE`:

**Option A**: Make `%DEFINE NAME VALUE;` perform text substitution when `NAME` appears as an identifier in expressions. This is closest to C's `#define` and most intuitive.

**Option B**: Add a new `%CONST NAME VALUE;` directive that explicitly signals "this is a substitution constant" vs the existing `%DEFINE` which remains conditional-only.

Either way, the substitution should happen at the lexer level (before parsing), replacing the identifier token with the value token.

## Motivation

The SNOBOL4 project (sw-cor24-snobol4) needs many tag/kind/flag constants shared across modules via `.msw` includes. Using `DCL STATIC INIT` for each wastes static storage for pure constants. With ~30+ constants, this adds up on a 1MB COR24 target.
===SEPARATOR===
title:	MACRODEF and DCL cannot coexist in the same .msw include file
state:	OPEN
author:	softwarewrighter (Software Wrighter LLC)
labels:	
comments:	1
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
number:	2
--
## Problem

When an `.msw` file contains both `DCL` statements (e.g., BASED records, STATIC constants) and `MACRODEF` blocks, the compiler gives:

```
SYNTAX ERROR line N: unexpected top-level token
```

at the first `MACRODEF` encountered after `DCL` statements.

**Works** (macros only):
```msw
/* greet.msw */
MACRODEF GREET;
    REQUIRED MSG(lvalue);
    GEN DO; 'la r0,{MSG}'; ... END;
END;
```

**Works** (declarations only):
```msw
/* cvt.msw */
DCL 1 CVT BASED,
    3 CVTEYE(4) CHAR,
    3 CVTVER BYTE;
```

**Fails** (mixed):
```msw
/* descr.msw */
DCL 1 DESCR BASED,
    3 DTAG BYTE,
    3 DPAY PTR;

MACRODEF BUILD_NULL;        /* <-- SYNTAX ERROR here */
    REQUIRED PTR(expr);
    GEN DO; 'la r2,{PTR}'; ... END;
END;
```

## Impact

This forces splitting every module into two files:
- `descr.msw` -- BASED records and DCL constants
- `descr_macros.msw` -- MACRODEF blocks

For a project like SNOBOL4 with many modules, this doubles the number of include files and uses more of the 12KB include buffer.

## How PL/I handles this

PL/I's `%INCLUDE` inserts text verbatim -- the included file can contain any mix of declarations, preprocessor directives, and executable code. There are no restrictions on content type within an include.

## Proposed fix

**Option A** (minimal): Allow the parser to accept `MACRODEF` as a valid top-level token during include expansion, same as it accepts `DCL`.

**Option B** (accept the split): Document that `.msw` files should contain either declarations OR macros, not both. This is a reasonable convention -- PL/SW's macro system generates inline assembly which is fundamentally different from data declarations, so separating them has some conceptual clarity.

If Option B, the convention should be documented and the error message improved (e.g., "MACRODEF not allowed after DCL in include file -- use a separate .msw file").

## Recommendation

Option A is preferable if straightforward. The parser likely just needs to check for `MACRODEF` in the same place it checks for `DCL` at top level during include processing.
===SEPARATOR===
title:	Increase include buffer size (12KB too small for multi-module projects)
state:	OPEN
author:	softwarewrighter (Software Wrighter LLC)
labels:	
comments:	0
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
number:	3
--
## Problem

The include buffer (`INC_BUF_SIZE`) is currently 12,288 bytes, shared across ALL registered `.msw` files. For a project with many shared declarations, this limit is easily hit.

```c
#define INC_BUF_SIZE 12288    /* src/main.c:5901 */
```

The SNOBOL4 project has 3 include files (descr.msw, heap.msw, trace.msw) totaling ~5KB in compact form. As more modules are added (allocator, pattern subsystem, AM definitions, symbol table), the buffer will fill up. Before trimming comments, the initial 3 files were ~15KB and triggered:

```
ERROR: include buffer overflow (content)
```

## Context

The compiler runs on the COR24 emulator with 1MB of SRAM. The current 12KB is conservative.

## Proposed fix

Increase `INC_BUF_SIZE` to 32768 (32KB) or 65536 (64KB). The compiler's memory usage is dominated by the AST pool and source buffer, so this should have minimal impact on total footprint.

Alternatively, a dynamically-sized approach using the existing arena allocator could remove the hard limit entirely.

content.text: Now let me check the saga plan structure and how to add steps.

content.content:
# PL/SW Compiler -- Implementation Plan

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Description                     | Deliverable                              |
|-------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 0     | Project setup and toolchain     | Build system, hello-world on emulator    |
| 1     | Lexer and token infrastructure  | Tokenize PL/SW source                   |
| 2     | Parser and AST                  | Parse core language constructs           |
| 3     | Symbol table and type system    | Scoped symbols, type checking            |
| 4     | Code generation basics          | Emit .s for simple programs              |
| 5     | Procedures and calling conv.    | PROC, CALL, RETURN with stack frames     |
| 6     | Control flow                    | IF/THEN/ELSE, DO WHILE, DO count        |
| 7     | Records and arrays              | Level-based structures, array access     |
| 8     | Pointers and addresses          | PTR type, address-of, dereference        |
| 9     | Inline assembly                 | ASM DO blocks, naked procedures          |
| 10    | Macro preprocessor              | .msw parsing, ?MACRO expansion, GEN      |
| 11    | Demo programs and testing       | UART hello, LED toggle, service wrappers |
| 12    | Polish and documentation        | Listings, error messages, docs           |

---

## Phase 0: Project Setup and Toolchain Validation

### Step 0.1: Repository structure
- Create `src/` for compiler C source
- Create `test/` for test PL/SW programs
- Create `examples/` for demo programs
- Create build script for tc24r + cor24-run pipeline
- Verify tc24r compiles a trivial C program
- Verify cor24-run assembles and executes it

### Step 0.2: UART I/O bootstrap
- Write uart_putchar, uart_getchar, uart_puts, uart_getline
- Write print_int for decimal output
- Test: compiler shell prints banner and reads input

### Step 0.3: String and memory utilities
- Write strcmp, strcpy, strlen, memset, memcpy
- Write simple arena allocator for compiler tables
- Test: basic string operations work

---

## Phase 1: Lexer and Token Infrastructure

### Step 1.1: Token types and keyword table
- Define token types: DCL, PROC, IF, THEN, ELSE, DO, WHILE, END,
  CALL, RETURN, ASM, IDENT, NUM, operators, punctuation
- Keyword lookup table (DCL, DECLARE, PROC, IF, THEN, ...)
- Test: identify keywords vs identifiers

### Step 1.2: Lexer implementation
- Scan input buffer character by character
- Handle: identifiers, numbers, string literals, operators
- Skip whitespace and comments (/* ... */ style)
- Handle multi-character operators (<=, >=, !=, ->)
- Test: tokenize sample PL/SW fragments

---

## Phase 2: Parser and AST

### Step 2.1: AST node pool and types
- Pre-allocated node pool (256 nodes)
- Node kinds: PROGRAM, DCL, PROC, IF, DO_WHILE, ASSIGN,
  BINOP, UNOP, CALL, RETURN, LITERAL, IDENT, ASM_BLOCK
- Allocator and tree-building helpers

### Step 2.2: Declaration parser
- Parse DCL statements with type attributes
- Parse level-based structure declarations
- Parse storage class attributes (STATIC, AUTOMATIC, EXTERNAL)
- Test: parse various DCL forms

### Step 2.3: Expression parser
- Binary operators: +, -, *, arithmetic
- Comparison operators: =, <, >, <=, >=, !=
- Logical operators: &, |, ^, ~ (bitwise)
- Shift operators: << >> (or SHL, SHR keywords)
- Parenthesized sub-expressions
- Operator precedence (standard arithmetic precedence)
- Test: parse expression trees

### Step 2.4: Statement parser
- Assignment: `var = expr;`
- IF/THEN/ELSE
- DO WHILE, DO count
- CALL proc(args)
- RETURN(expr)
- DO; ... END; blocks
- Test: parse compound statements

### Step 2.5: Procedure parser
- PROC name OPTIONS(...); ... END;
- Parameter declarations
- Local declarations
- RETURNS(type) clause
- Test: parse complete procedures

### Step 2.6: Top-level parser
- Parse sequence of top-level DCL and PROC
- Build program AST
- Test: parse multi-procedure programs

---

## Phase 3: Symbol Table and Type System

### Step 3.1: Symbol table
- Scoped symbol table (global + per-procedure)
- Insert, lookup, scope enter/exit
- Track: name, type, storage class, offset, level
- Test: nested scopes resolve correctly

### Step 3.2: Type representation
- Internal type tags: INT8, INT16, INT24, BYTE, CHAR, PTR,
  ARRAY, RECORD
- Width and signedness attributes
- Record field lists with offsets
- Test: type construction and comparison

### Step 3.3: Type checking
- Expression type inference and checking
- Assignment compatibility
- Procedure argument/return type checking
- Pointer type validation
- Test: type errors detected correctly

### Step 3.4: Storage layout
- Compute record sizes and field offsets from level declarations
- Compute array sizes
- Assign stack offsets for AUTOMATIC variables
- Assign static addresses for STATIC variables
- Test: layout matches expected sizes

---

## Phase 4: Code Generation Basics

### Step 4.1: Assembly emitter framework
- Output buffer and emit functions
- Section management (.text, .data)
- Label generation (unique labels for branches)
- Comment emission (source line tracking)
- Test: emit skeleton .s file

### Step 4.2: Expression codegen
- Literal loading (mvi)
- Variable load/store (lw/sw with offsets)
- Binary arithmetic (add, sub, mul)
- Software divide routine
- Comparison to branch conditions
- Register allocation for expression temporaries
- Test: evaluate expressions, print results via UART

### Step 4.3: Assignment codegen
- Simple variable assignment
- Record field assignment (base + offset)
- Array element assignment (base + index * size)
- Test: assign and read back values

### Step 4.4: Static data emission
- String literals in .data section
- Static variable allocation
- Initial values
- Test: static data accessible at runtime

---

## Phase 5: Procedures and Calling Convention

### Step 5.1: Procedure prologue/epilogue
- Push fp, save callee-saved registers
- Set up frame pointer
- Allocate stack space for locals
- Epilogue: restore and return
- Test: empty procedure call/return works

### Step 5.2: Procedure calls
- Evaluate arguments, push right-to-left
- CALL instruction
- Clean up stack after return
- Capture return value from r0
- Test: call with arguments, get return value

### Step 5.3: Nested calls and recursion
- Correct frame management for nested calls
- Register save/restore across calls
- Test: recursive factorial

---

## Phase 6: Control Flow

### Step 6.1: IF/THEN/ELSE
- Evaluate condition
- Conditional branch to else/end label
- Test: if/then, if/then/else

### Step 6.2: DO WHILE loop
- Loop header label
- Condition evaluation and branch
- Loop body
- Branch back to header
- Test: counted loop via while

### Step 6.3: DO count loop (iteration)
- Loop variable, start, end, optional increment
- Lowered to while-equivalent
- Test: DO I = 1 TO 10

---

## Phase 7: Records and Arrays

### Step 7.1: Record field access
- Dot notation: `rec.field`
- Computed offset from record layout
- Nested record access: `rec.sub.field`
- Test: declare, assign, read record fields

### Step 7.2: Array access
- Index expression: `arr(i)`
- Bounds checking (optional, controlled by option)
- Multi-dimensional arrays (future)
- Test: declare, fill, read array

---

## Phase 8: Pointers and Addresses

### Step 8.1: Pointer operations
- ADDR(var) -- address-of operator
- PTR-> dereference
- Pointer arithmetic (add offset)
- Test: pointer to record, pointer to array element

### Step 8.2: BASED storage (future)
- Variables accessed via explicit base pointer
- Overlay semantics
- Test: control block access via pointer

---

## Phase 9: Inline Assembly

### Step 9.1: ASM DO blocks
- Parse ASM DO; "string"; ... END;
- Emit assembly strings verbatim into .s output
- String interpolation for variable references
- Test: inline asm that manipulates registers

### Step 9.2: Naked procedures
- PROC with OPTIONS(NAKED) skips prologue/epilogue
- Entire body is ASM
- Test: interrupt handler stub

---

## Phase 10: Macro Preprocessor

### Step 10.1: Include processing
- %INCLUDE directive
- Search path from -I flags
- Read and insert .msw file contents
- Test: include a .msw file

### Step 10.2: Macro definition parsing
- Parse MACRODEF ... END; in .msw files
- Store macro name, clause grammar, body
- REQUIRED and OPTIONAL clauses
- Test: parse a macro definition

### Step 10.3: Macro expansion
- Recognize ?MACRO(...) in source
- Match keyword arguments to clauses
- Substitute arguments into macro body
- Expand to PL/SW source tokens
- Test: ?GETMAIN expands correctly

### Step 10.4: GEN blocks
- GEN DO; ... END; within macro bodies
- Emit assembly directly during expansion
- Template substitution for arguments
- Test: macro with GEN emits correct assembly

### Step 10.5: Conditional compilation
- %IF / %THEN / %ELSE / %ENDIF
- Based on defined symbols or target flags
- Test: conditional include/exclude

---

## Phase 11: Demo Programs and Testing

### Step 11.1: UART hello world
- PL/SW program that prints "Hello from PL/SW!" via UART
- End-to-end: .plsw -> compiler -> .s -> cor24-run

### Step 11.2: LED toggle
- MMIO write to LED address using inline ASM
- Test on emulator

### Step 11.3: Counted loop with output
- Loop printing numbers 1..10
- Demonstrates DO, CALL, arithmetic

### Step 11.4: Record and pointer demo
- Declare a record, fill fields, access via pointer
- Demonstrates level-based DCL, PTR, field access

### Step 11.5: Macro demo
- .msw file with service macro
- .plsw file invoking macro
- Demonstrates macro pipeline

---

## Phase 12: Polish and Documentation

### Step 12.1: Mixed listing output
- Source line + generated assembly interleaved
- Controlled by compiler flag

### Step 12.2: Error messages
- Line numbers in all error messages
- Descriptive error categories

### Step 12.3: Final documentation
- Update README with usage, examples
- Document language syntax reference
- Document macro system reference
- Example session transcripts

content.content:
Workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track

Usage: agentrail [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  init      Initialize a new saga
  setup     Bootstrap a project: create saga, CLAUDE.md, and register domain
  add       Add a step to the saga (for maintenance mode or ad-hoc tasks)
  status    Show current saga state
  next      Output current step prompt and context for a fresh agent session
  begin     Mark the current step as in-progress
  complete  Complete current step, optionally define next step
  plan      View or update the saga plan
  history   Show all step summaries
  distill   Distill trajectories into a skill document
  run-loop  Auto-execute deterministic steps, pause at agent steps
  abort     Mark current step as blocked
  archive   Archive current saga to .agentrail-archive/ and clear .agentrail/
  help      Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
      --saga <SAGA>  Path to the project directory (default: current directory) [default: .]
  -h, --help         Print help
  -V, --version      Print version

content.text: Now I'll add three new steps, one per issue.

input.command: agentrail add --slug "define-substitution" --prompt "Investigate and implement %DEFINE value substitution in expressions (GitHub issue #1). Currently %DEFINE NAME VALUE only works for %IF DEFINED(NAME) but does not substitute VALUE when NAME appears in expressions. The goal is compile-time constants with zero runtime cost. Investigate the lexer's existing %DEFINE handling, then implement Option A: make %DEFINE NAME VALUE perform text substitution at the lexer level when NAME appears as an identifier token. When the lexer produces an identifier, check the define table; if found and the define has a value, replace the identifier token with the value token. Update the chain example or add a new example demonstrating %DEFINE constants. Reference: PL/I uses %DCL/%ACTIVATE but a simpler single-step approach is preferred for PL/SW."

content.content: Advanced to step 041-define-substitution.

toolUseResult.stdout: Advanced to step 041-define-substitution.

input.command: agentrail add --slug "mixed-msw-includes" --prompt "Investigate and fix MACRODEF and DCL coexistence in .msw include files (GitHub issue #2). Currently, including an .msw file that has both DCL statements and MACRODEF blocks causes 'SYNTAX ERROR: unexpected top-level token' at the first MACRODEF after DCL. This forces splitting every module into two files (one for declarations, one for macros), which doubles include files and wastes include buffer space. Investigate the top-level parser's include processing path to understand why MACRODEF is not accepted after DCL during include expansion. The fix is likely allowing the parser to check for MACRODEF in the same place it checks for DCL at top level during include processing. Implement the fix (Option A preferred), add a test case with mixed DCL+MACRODEF in one .msw file, and verify existing examples still work."

agentrail add --slug "increase-include-buffer" --prompt "Increase the include buffer size from 12KB to a larger value (GitHub issue #3). The current INC_BUF_SIZE of 12288 bytes is too small for multi-module projects. The SNOBOL4 project already has 3 include files totaling ~5KB in compact form, and before trimming comments they were ~15KB which triggered 'ERROR: include buffer overflow'. The COR24 has 1MB SRAM so 12KB is overly conservative. Find INC_BUF_SIZE in src/main.c and increase it to 32768 (32KB). This is a simple constant change. Verify the compiler still builds and existing examples work after the change."

content.content: Added step 042-mixed-msw-includes.

toolUseResult.stdout: Added step 042-mixed-msw-includes.

content.content: Added step 043-increase-include-buffer.

toolUseResult.stdout: Added step 043-increase-include-buffer.

content.text:
Done. Three new saga steps added:

- **041-define-substitution** — Implement `%DEFINE` value substitution in expressions (issue #1). Lexer-level text replacement so `%DEFINE TAG_NULL 0;` makes `TAG_NULL` usable in expressions as a compile-time constant.
- **042-mixed-msw-includes** — Fix parser to accept both `DCL` and `MACRODEF` in the same `.msw` file (issue #2). Likely a small parser change to recognize `MACRODEF` as valid top-level during include processing.
- **043-increase-include-buffer** — Bump `INC_BUF_SIZE` from 12KB to 32KB (issue #3). Simple constant change, plenty of headroom in 1MB SRAM.

- Branch back to header
- Test: counted loop via while

### Step 6.3: DO count loop (iteration)
- Loop variable, start, end, optional increment
- Lowered to while-equivalent
- Test: DO I = 1 TO 10

---

## Phase 7: Records and Arrays

### Step 7.1: Record field access
- Dot notation: `rec.field`
- Computed offset from record layout
- Nested record access: `rec.sub.field`
- Test: declare, assign, read record fields

### Step 7.2: Array access
- Index expression: `arr(i)`
- Bounds checking (optional, controlled by option)
- Multi-dimensional arrays (future)
- Test: declare, fill, read array

---

## Phase 8: Pointers and Addresses

### Step 8.1: Pointer operations
- ADDR(var) -- address-of operator
- PTR-> dereference
- Pointer arithmetic (add offset)
- Test: pointer to record, pointer to array element

### Step 8.2: BASED storage (future)
- Variables accessed via explicit base pointer
- Overlay semantics
- Test: control block access via pointer

---

## Phase 9: Inline Assembly

### Step 9.1: ASM DO blocks
- Parse ASM DO; "string"; ... END;
- Emit assembly strings verbatim into .s output
- String interpolation for variable references
- Test: inline asm that manipulates registers

### Step 9.2: Naked procedures
- PROC with OPTIONS(NAKED) skips prologue/epilogue
- Entire body is ASM
- Test: interrupt handler stub

---

## Phase 10: Macro Preprocessor

### Step 10.1: Include processing
- %INCLUDE directive
- Search path from -I flags
- Read and insert .msw file contents
- Test: include a .msw file

### Step 10.2: Macro definition parsing
- Parse MACRODEF ... END; in .msw files
- Store macro name, clause grammar, body
- REQUIRED and OPTIONAL clauses
- Test: parse a macro definition

### Step 10.3: Macro expansion
- Recognize ?MACRO(...) in source
- Match keyword arguments to clauses
- Substitute arguments into macro body
- Expand to PL/SW source tokens
- Test: ?GETMAIN expands correctly

### Step 10.4: GEN blocks
- GEN DO; ... END; within macro bodies
- Emit assembly directly during expansion
- Template substitution for arguments
- Test: macro with GEN emits correct assembly

### Step 10.5: Conditional compilation
- %IF / %THEN / %ELSE / %ENDIF
- Based on defined symbols or target flags
- Test: conditional include/exclude

---

## Phase 11: Demo Programs and Testing

### Step 11.1: UART hello world
- PL/SW program that prints "Hello from PL/SW!" via UART

- MMIO write to LED address using inline ASM

- Loop printing numbers 1..10

- Declare a record, fill fields, access via pointer
- Demonstrates level-based DCL, PTR, field access

### Step 11.5: Macro demo
- .msw file with service macro
- .plsw file invoking macro
- Demonstrates macro pipeline

---

## Phase 12: Polish and Documentation

### Step 12.1: Mixed listing output
- Source line + generated assembly interleaved
- Controlled by compiler flag

### Step 12.2: Error messages
- Line numbers in all error messages
- Descriptive error categories

### Step 12.3: Final documentation
- Update README with usage, examples
- Document language syntax reference
- Document macro system reference
- Example session transcripts

Investigate and implement %DEFINE value substitution in expressions (GitHub issue #1). Currently %DEFINE NAME VALUE only works for %IF DEFINED(NAME) but does not substitute VALUE when NAME appears in expressions. The goal is compile-time constants with zero runtime cost. Investigate the lexer's existing %DEFINE handling, then implement Option A: make %DEFINE NAME VALUE perform text substitution at the lexer level when NAME appears as an identifier token. When the lexer produces an identifier, check the define table; if found and the define has a value, replace the identifier token with the value token. Update the chain example or add a new example demonstrating %DEFINE constants. Reference: PL/I uses %DCL/%ACTIVATE but a simpler single-step approach is preferred for PL/SW.

=== PLAN ===
# PL/SW Compiler -- Implementation Plan

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Description                     | Deliverable                              |
|-------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 0     | Project setup and toolchain     | Build system, hello-world on emulator    |
| 1     | Lexer and token infrastructure  | Tokenize PL/SW source                   |
| 2     | Parser and AST                  | Parse core language constructs           |
| 3     | Symbol table and type system    | Scoped symbols, type checking            |
| 4     | Code generation basics          | Emit .s for simple programs              |
| 5     | Procedures and calling conv.    | PROC, CALL, RETURN with stack frames     |
| 6     | Control flow                    | IF/THEN/ELSE, DO WHILE, DO count        |
| 7     | Records and arrays              | Level-based structures, array access     |
| 8     | Pointers and addresses          | PTR type, address-of, dereference        |
| 9     | Inline assembly                 | ASM DO blocks, naked procedures          |
| 10    | Macro preprocessor              | .msw parsing, ?MACRO expansion, GEN      |
| 11    | Demo programs and testing       | UART hello, LED toggle, service wrappers |
| 12    | Polish and documentation        | Listings, error messages, docs           |

---

## Phase 0: Project Setup and Toolchain Validation

### Step 0.1: Repository structure
- Create `src/` for compiler C source
- Create `test/` for test PL/SW programs
- Create `examples/` for demo programs
- Create build script for tc24r + cor24-run pipeline
- Verify tc24r compiles a trivial C program
- Verify cor24-run assembles and executes it

### Step 0.2: UART I/O bootstrap
- Write uart_putchar, uart_getchar, uart_puts, uart_getline
- Write print_int for decimal output
- Test: compiler shell prints banner and reads input

### Step 0.3: String and memory utilities
- Write strcmp, strcpy, strlen, memset, memcpy
- Write simple arena allocator for compiler tables
- Test: basic string operations work

---

## Phase 1: Lexer and Token Infrastructure

### Step 1.1: Token types and keyword table
- Define token types: DCL, PROC, IF, THEN, ELSE, DO, WHILE, END,
  CALL, RETURN, ASM, IDENT, NUM, operators, punctuation
- Keyword lookup table (DCL, DECLARE, PROC, IF, THEN, ...)
- Test: identify keywords vs identifiers

### Step 1.2: Lexer implementation
- Scan input buffer character by character
- Handle: identifiers, numbers, string literals, operators
- Skip whitespace and comments (/* ... */ style)
- Handle multi-character operators (<=, >=, !=, ->)
- Test: tokenize sample PL/SW fragments

---

## Phase 2: Parser and AST

### Step 2.1: AST node pool and types
- Pre-allocated node pool (256 nodes)
- Node kinds: PROGRAM, DCL, PROC, IF, DO_WHILE, ASSIGN,
  BINOP, UNOP, CALL, RETURN, LITERAL, IDENT, ASM_BLOCK
- Allocator and tree-building helpers

### Step 2.2: Declaration parser
- Parse DCL statements with type attributes
- Parse level-based structure declarations
- Parse storage class attributes (STATIC, AUTOMATIC, EXTERNAL)
- Test: parse various DCL forms

### Step 2.3: Expression parser
- Binary operators: +, -, *, arithmetic
- Comparison operators: =, <, >, <=, >=, !=
- Logical operators: &, |, ^, ~ (bitwise)
- Shift operators: << >> (or SHL, SHR keywords)
- Parenthesized sub-expressions
- Operator precedence (standard arithmetic precedence)
- Test: parse expression trees

### Step 2.4: Statement parser
- Assignment: `var = expr;`
- IF/THEN/ELSE
- DO WHILE, DO count
- CALL proc(args)
- RETURN(expr)
- DO; ... END; blocks
- Test: parse compound statements

### Step 2.5: Procedure parser
- PROC name OPTIONS(...); ... END;
- Parameter declarations
- Local declarations
- RETURNS(type) clause
- Test: parse complete procedures

### Step 2.6: Top-level parser
- Parse sequence of top-level DCL and PROC
- Build program AST
- Test: parse multi-procedure programs

---

## Phase 3: Symbol Table and Type System

### Step 3.1: Symbol table
- Scoped symbol table (global + per-procedure)
- Insert, lookup, scope enter/exit
- Track: name, type, storage class, offset, level
- Test: nested scopes resolve correctly

### Step 3.2: Type representation
- Internal type tags: INT8, INT16, INT24, BYTE, CHAR, PTR,
  ARRAY, RECORD
- Width and signedness attributes
- Record field lists with offsets
- Test: type construction and comparison

### Step 3.3: Type checking
- Expression type inference and checking
- Assignment compatibility
- Procedure argument/return type checking
- Pointer type validation
- Test: type errors detected correctly

### Step 3.4: Storage layout
- Compute record sizes and field offsets from level declarations
- Compute array sizes
- Assign stack offsets for AUTOMATIC variables
- Assign static addresses for STATIC variables
- Test: layout matches expected sizes

---

## Phase 4: Code Generation Basics

### Step 4.1: Assembly emitter framework
- Output buffer and emit functions
- Section management (.text, .data)
- Label generation (unique labels for branches)
- Comment emission (source line tracking)
- Test: emit skeleton .s file

### Step 4.2: Expression codegen
- Literal loading (mvi)
- Variable load/store (lw/sw with offsets)
- Binary arithmetic (add, sub, mul)
- Software divide routine
- Comparison to branch conditions
- Register allocation for expression temporaries
- Test: evaluate expressions, print results via UART

### Step 4.3: Assignment codegen
- Simple variable assignment
- Record field assignment (base + offset)
- Array element assignment (base + index * size)
- Test: assign and read back values

### Step 4.4: Static data emission
- String literals in .data section
- Static variable allocation
- Initial values
- Test: static data accessible at runtime

---

## Phase 5: Procedures and Calling Convention

### Step 5.1: Procedure prologue/epilogue
- Push fp, save callee-saved registers
- Set up frame pointer
- Allocate stack space for locals
- Epilogue: restore and return
- Test: empty procedure call/return works

### Step 5.2: Procedure calls
- Evaluate arguments, push right-to-left
- CALL instruction
- Clean up stack after return
- Capture return value from r0
- Test: call with arguments, get return value

### Step 5.3: Nested calls and recursion
- Correct frame management for nested calls
- Register save/restore across calls
- Test: recursive factorial

---

## Phase 6: Control Flow

### Step 6.1: IF/THEN/ELSE
- Evaluate condition
- Conditional branch to else/end label
- Test: if/then, if/then/else

### Step 6.2: DO WHILE loop
- Loop header label
- Condition evaluation and branch
- Loop body
- Branch back to header
- Test: counted loop via while

### Step 6.3: DO count loop (iteration)
- Loop variable, start, end, optional increment
- Lowered to while-equivalent
- Test: DO I = 1 TO 10

---

## Phase 7: Records and Arrays

### Step 7.1: Record field access
- Dot notation: `rec.field`
- Computed offset from record layout
- Nested record access: `rec.sub.field`
- Test: declare, assign, read record fields

### Step 7.2: Array access
- Index expression: `arr(i)`
- Bounds checking (optional, controlled by option)
- Multi-dimensional arrays (future)
- Test: declare, fill, read array

---

## Phase 8: Pointers and Addresses

### Step 8.1: Pointer operations
- ADDR(var) -- address-of operator
- PTR-> dereference
- Pointer arithmetic (add offset)
- Test: pointer to record, pointer to array element

### Step 8.2: BASED storage (future)
- Variables accessed via explicit base pointer
- Overlay semantics
- Test: control block access via pointer

---

## Phase 9: Inline Assembly

### Step 9.1: ASM DO blocks
- Parse ASM DO; "string"; ... END;
- Emit assembly strings verbatim into .s output
- String interpolation for variable references
- Test: inline asm that manipulates registers

### Step 9.2: Naked procedures
- PROC with OPTIONS(NAKED) skips prologue/epilogue
- Entire body is ASM
- Test: interrupt handler stub

---

## Phase 10: Macro Preprocessor

### Step 10.1: Include processing
- %INCLUDE directive
- Search path from -I flags
- Read and insert .msw file contents
- Test: include a .msw file

### Step 10.2: Macro definition parsing
- Parse MACRODEF ... END; in .msw files
- Store macro name, clause grammar, body
- REQUIRED and OPTIONAL clauses
- Test: parse a macro definition

### Step 10.3: Macro expansion
- Recognize ?MACRO(...) in source
- Match keyword arguments to clauses
- Substitute arguments into macro body
- Expand to PL/SW source tokens
- Test: ?GETMAIN expands correctly

### Step 10.4: GEN blocks
- GEN DO; ... END; within macro bodies
- Emit assembly directly during expansion
- Template substitution for arguments
- Test: macro with GEN emits correct assembly

### Step 10.5: Conditional compilation
- %IF / %THEN / %ELSE / %ENDIF
- Based on defined symbols or target flags
- Test: conditional include/exclude

---

## Phase 11: Demo Programs and Testing

### Step 11.1: UART hello world
- PL/SW program that prints "Hello from PL/SW!" via UART
- End-to-end: .plsw -> compiler -> .s -> cor24-run

### Step 11.2: LED toggle
- MMIO write to LED address using inline ASM
- Test on emulator

### Step 11.3: Counted loop with output
- Loop printing numbers 1..10
- Demonstrates DO, CALL, arithmetic

### Step 11.4: Record and pointer demo
- Declare a record, fill fields, access via pointer
- Demonstrates level-based DCL, PTR, field access

### Step 11.5: Macro demo
- .msw file with service macro
- .plsw file invoking macro
- Demonstrates macro pipeline

---

## Phase 12: Polish and Documentation

### Step 12.1: Mixed listing output
- Source line + generated assembly interleaved
- Controlled by compiler flag

### Step 12.2: Error messages
- Line numbers in all error messages
- Descriptive error categories

### Step 12.3: Final documentation
- Update README with usage, examples
- Document language syntax reference
- Document macro system reference
- Example session transcripts

file.content:
# PL/SW Compiler -- Build System
# Builds plsw compiler (C via tc24r) for COR24 emulator

tc24r_include := env("HOME") / "github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r/include"
main_c := "src/main.c"
main_s := "build/plsw.s"

# Build the compiler
build:
    tc24r {{main_c}} -o {{main_s}} -I {{tc24r_include}} -I src

# Build and run interactively
run: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --terminal --echo --speed 0

# Build and run with UART input string (supports \n escapes)
run-input input: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --speed 0 -u "{{input}}"

# Build and run with cycle limit (for testing)
test: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --terminal --speed 0 -n 100000000

# Compile and run a .plsw program end-to-end
# Usage: just pipeline examples/hello.plsw
#        just pipeline examples/greet.msw examples/hello_macro.plsw
pipeline *args: build
    ./scripts/pipeline.sh {{args}}

# Compile a .plsw, save .s, and run with --dump for memory inspection
# Output: build/out.s (assembly) and build/run-dump.txt (memory dump)
pipeline-dump *args: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh {{args}}

# Compile hello_macro example (macro demo)
hello-macro: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh examples/greet.msw examples/hello_macro.plsw

# Compile chain example (control block demo with .msw includes)
chain: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh include/cvt.msw include/ascb.msw include/asxb.msw include/tcb.msw examples/chain.plsw

# Clean build artifacts
clean:
    rm -f build/*.s build/*-combined.plsw build/*-dump.txt

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
# pipeline.sh -- End-to-end PL/SW compilation and execution pipeline
#
# Usage: ./scripts/pipeline.sh [macro.msw ...] program.plsw
#
# When .msw files are provided, uses the FILE:/SOURCE: protocol so the
# compiler's %INCLUDE mechanism resolves them by name. Without .msw files,
# uses legacy mode (raw source + EOT).
#
# Requires: cor24-run, the compiler assembly at build/plsw.s

set -euo pipefail

COMPILER_ASM="${COMPILER_ASM:-build/plsw.s}"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 [macro.msw ...] program.plsw" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Verify compiler exists
if [ ! -f "$COMPILER_ASM" ]; then
    echo "Error: compiler not built. Run 'just build' first." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Separate .msw and .plsw files
MACROS=()
MAIN=""
for f in "$@"; do
    case "$f" in
        *.msw) MACROS+=("$f") ;;
        *.plsw) MAIN="$f" ;;
        *) echo "Error: unknown file type: $f" >&2; exit 1 ;;
    esac
done

if [ -z "$MAIN" ]; then
    echo "Error: no .plsw file specified" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Build UART input string with \n escapes for cor24-run -u
# Uses FILE:/SOURCE: protocol when .msw files present, legacy otherwise.
build_input() {
    printf 'c\\n'
    if [ ${#MACROS[@]} -gt 0 ]; then
        # FILE: protocol -- each .msw becomes a named include
        for m in "${MACROS[@]}"; do
            printf 'FILE:%s\\n' "$(basename "$m")"
            while IFS= read -r line; do
                printf '%s\\n' "$line"
            done < "$m"
            printf '\\x1E'  # record separator = end of file content
        done
        printf 'SOURCE:\\n'
    fi
    while IFS= read -r line; do
        printf '%s\\n' "$line"
    done < "$MAIN"
    printf '\\x04'
}

INPUT=$(build_input)

# Compile: run source through the PL/SW compiler on the emulator
COMPILER_OUT=$(cor24-run --run "$COMPILER_ASM" -u "$INPUT" -n 200000000 -t 120 --speed 0 2>&1)

# Extract the UART output block (multiline: from "UART output:" to "Executed")
UART_OUT=$(echo "$COMPILER_OUT" | sed -n '/^UART output:/,/^Executed /{/^Executed /d;p;}' | sed '1s/^UART output: //')

if echo "$UART_OUT" | grep -q "compilation failed\|COMPILE ERROR\|ERROR:"; then
    echo "Compilation failed:" >&2
    echo "$UART_OUT" | grep -E "ERROR:|failed" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Extract assembly between markers
START_MARKER="--- generated assembly ---"
END_MARKER="--- end assembly ---"
ASM=$(echo "$UART_OUT" | sed -n "/$START_MARKER/,/$END_MARKER/{/$START_MARKER/d;/$END_MARKER/d;p;}")

if [ -z "$ASM" ]; then
    echo "Error: no assembly output found in compiler output" >&2
    echo "Compiler output (last 20 lines):" >&2
    echo "$UART_OUT" | tail -20 >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Write assembly to temp file
TMPASM=$(mktemp /tmp/plsw-XXXXXX.s)
trap "rm -f $TMPASM" EXIT
echo "$ASM" > "$TMPASM"

ASM_LINES=$(echo "$ASM" | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
echo "=== Compiled $(basename "$MAIN") ($ASM_LINES lines of assembly) ===" >&2

# Show registered includes if any
echo "$UART_OUT" | grep "registered:" >&2 || true

# Run the generated assembly
echo "=== Running ===" >&2
RUN_OUT=$(cor24-run --run "$TMPASM" -n 10000000 -t 30 --speed 0 2>&1)

# Extract program output (multiline UART output block)
PROG_OUT=$(echo "$RUN_OUT" | sed -n '/^UART output:/,/^Executed /{/^Executed /d;p;}' | sed '1s/^UART output: //')

if [ -n "$PROG_OUT" ]; then
    echo "$PROG_OUT"
else
    echo "(no output)" >&2
fi

content.text:
# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# sw-cor24-plsw

PL/SW (Programming Language for Software Wrighter) compiler for the
COR24 24-bit RISC ISA.

A freestanding, PL/I-inspired systems language with compile-time macro
metaprogramming, BASED record templates, and controlled inline assembly.
Transpiles to human-readable COR24 assembler source (.s) with
interleaved source-line comments.

## Status: v1 Complete

All 40 implementation steps complete across 12 phases:

- **Lexer** -- tokenizer with keywords, operators, string/number literals
- **Parser** -- recursive descent for DCL, PROC, IF, DO WHILE, DO count
- **AST** -- 256-node pool with 18 node kinds
- **Symbol table** -- scoped (global + per-procedure), type tracking
- **Type system** -- INT(8/16/24), BYTE, CHAR, PTR, WORD, BIT, RECORD
- **Storage layout** -- stack frames, static data, record field offsets
- **Code generation** -- COR24 assembly for all constructs
- **Inline assembly** -- ASM DO blocks, NAKED procedures
- **Macros** -- MACRODEF/GEN, %INCLUDE, ?MACRO() invocation
- **Conditionals** -- %IF/%DEFINE/%ELSE/%ENDIF
- **Built-ins** -- ADDR(), SIZEOF(), string literal assignment to ptr->field
- **Listing** -- source lines as assembly comments, line numbers in errors
- **Pipeline** -- FILE: protocol for multi-file compilation via UART

## Quick Start

```bash
# Build the compiler (requires tc24r and cor24-run)
just build

# Compile and run hello world
just pipeline examples/hello.plsw
# Output: Hello from PL/SW!

# Compile with .msw includes and memory dump
just chain
# Output: build/chain.s, build/chain-dump.txt, build/chain-combined.plsw

# Compile macro demo
just hello-macro
# Output: Hello from macros!
```

## Examples

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `examples/hello.plsw` | Hello world via UART_PUTS |
| `examples/led.plsw` | LED MMIO toggle with inline ASM |
| `examples/loop.plsw` | DO I = 1 TO 10 with PRINT_INT |
| `examples/record.plsw` | Record fields and pointer dereference |
| `examples/chain.plsw` | Control block chain (CVT/ASCB/ASXB/TCB) with arena allocator |
| `examples/macro.plsw` | ?LED_SET and ?NOP macro invocation |
| `examples/hello_macro.plsw` | ?GREET macro via %INCLUDE |

## Pipeline

The compiler runs on the COR24 emulator. Source is fed via UART:

```
.plsw + .msw files  -->  pipeline.sh  -->  compiler on emulator
                                               |
                                           .s assembly
                                               |
                                          cor24-run --run  -->  program output
```

For multi-file compilation, the FILE:/SOURCE: protocol registers
named include files so `%INCLUDE name;` resolves them:

```bash
./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh include/cvt.msw include/ascb.msw examples/chain.plsw
```

Output files: `build/<name>.s`, `build/<name>-dump.txt`, `build/<name>-combined.plsw`

## Build System

```bash
just build          # Compile the PL/SW compiler itself
just run            # Interactive mode (terminal)
just test           # Run with instruction limit
just pipeline <f>   # Compile and run a .plsw
just pipeline-dump <files>  # Compile, run with memory dump
just chain          # Control block chain demo
just hello-macro    # Macro demo
just clean          # Remove build artifacts
```

## Documentation

- [Usage Guide & Language Reference](docs/usage.md)
- [Product Requirements](docs/prd.md)
- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md)
- [Design Decisions](docs/design.md)
- [Implementation Plan](docs/plan.md)
- [Research Notes](docs/research.txt)
- [Assembler Directive Report](docs/byte-comm.md)

## COR24 Target

- 24-bit RISC, 8 registers (r0-r2 GP, fp, sp, z, iv, ir)
- 1 MB SRAM + 8 KB EBR stack
- UART at 0xFF0100 (data), 0xFF0101 (status)
- LED at 0xFF0000 (active-low)
- Variable-length instructions (1, 2, or 4 bytes)
- Calling convention: args on stack R-to-L, return in r0

## License

See [LICENSE](LICENSE).

+{"parentUuid":"2aa4c281-95e1-41d7-b9c8-545821f41b2b","isSidechain":false,"promptId":"38cff907-bbaa-4a30-a277-cd8a6e913ca9","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_0192RAXcpttCHQG8o7sRyz7C","type":"tool_result","content":"=== PLAN ===
# PL/SW Compiler -- Implementation Plan

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Description                     | Deliverable                              |
|-------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 0     | Project setup and toolchain     | Build system, hello-world on emulator    |
| 1     | Lexer and token infrastructure  | Tokenize PL/SW source                   |
| 2     | Parser and AST                  | Parse core language constructs           |
| 3     | Symbol table and type system    | Scoped symbols, type checking            |
| 4     | Code generation basics          | Emit .s for simple programs              |
| 5     | Procedures and calling conv.    | PROC, CALL, RETURN with stack frames     |
| 6     | Control flow                    | IF/THEN/ELSE, DO WHILE, DO count        |
| 7     | Records and arrays              | Level-based structures, array access     |
| 8     | Pointers and addresses          | PTR type, address-of, dereference        |
| 9     | Inline assembly                 | ASM DO blocks, naked procedures          |
| 10    | Macro preprocessor              | .msw parsing, ?MACRO expansion, GEN      |
| 11    | Demo programs and testing       | UART hello, LED toggle, service wrappers |
| 12    | Polish and documentation        | Listings, error messages, docs           |

---

## Phase 0: Project Setup and Toolchain Validation

### Step 0.1: Repository structure
- Create `src/` for compiler C source
- Create `test/` for test PL/SW programs
- Create `examples/` for demo programs
- Create build script for tc24r + cor24-run pipeline
- Verify tc24r compiles a trivial C program
- Verify cor24-run assembles and executes it

### Step 0.2: UART I/O bootstrap
- Write uart_putchar, uart_getchar, uart_puts, uart_getline
- Write print_int for decimal output
- Test: compiler shell prints banner and reads input

### Step 0.3: String and memory utilities
- Write strcmp, strcpy, strlen, memset, memcpy
- Write simple arena allocator for compiler tables
- Test: basic string operations work

---

## Phase 1: Lexer and Token Infrastructure

### Step 1.1: Token types and keyword table
- Define token types: DCL, PROC, IF, THEN, ELSE, DO, WHILE, END,
  CALL, RETURN, ASM, IDENT, NUM, operators, punctuation
- Keyword lookup table (DCL, DECLARE, PROC, IF, THEN, ...)
- Test: identify keywords vs identifiers

### Step 1.2: Lexer implementation
- Scan input buffer character by character
- Handle: identifiers, numbers, string literals, operators
- Skip whitespace and comments (/* ... */ style)
- Handle multi-character operators (<=, >=, !=, ->)
- Test: tokenize sample PL/SW fragments

---

## Phase 2: Parser and AST

### Step 2.1: AST node pool and types
- Pre-allocated node pool (256 nodes)
- Node kinds: PROGRAM, DCL, PROC, IF, DO_WHILE, ASSIGN,
  BINOP, UNOP, CALL, RETURN, LITERAL, IDENT, ASM_BLOCK
- Allocator and tree-building helpers

### Step 2.2: Declaration parser
- Parse DCL statements with type attributes
- Parse level-based structure declarations
- Parse storage class attributes (STATIC, AUTOMATIC, EXTERNAL)
- Test: parse various DCL forms

### Step 2.3: Expression parser
- Binary operators: +, -, *, arithmetic
- Comparison operators: =, <, >, <=, >=, !=
- Logical operators: &, |, ^, ~ (bitwise)
- Shift operators: << >> (or SHL, SHR keywords)
- Parenthesized sub-expressions
- Operator precedence (standard arithmetic precedence)
- Test: parse expression trees

### Step 2.4: Statement parser
- Assignment: `var = expr;`
- IF/THEN/ELSE
- DO WHILE, DO count
- CALL proc(args)
- RETURN(expr)
- DO; ... END; blocks
- Test: parse compound statements

### Step 2.5: Procedure parser
- PROC name OPTIONS(...); ... END;
- Parameter declarations
- Local declarations
- RETURNS(type) clause
- Test: parse complete procedures

### Step 2.6: Top-level parser
- Parse sequence of top-level DCL and PROC
- Build program AST
- Test: parse multi-procedure programs

---

## Phase 3: Symbol Table and Type System

### Step 3.1: Symbol table
- Scoped symbol table (global + per-procedure)
- Insert, lookup, scope enter/exit
- Track: name, type, storage class, offset, level
- Test: nested scopes resolve correctly

### Step 3.2: Type representation
- Internal type tags: INT8, INT16, INT24, BYTE, CHAR, PTR,
  ARRAY, RECORD
- Width and signedness attributes
- Record field lists with offsets
- Test: type construction and comparison

### Step 3.3: Type checking
- Expression type inference and checking
- Assignment compatibility
- Procedure argument/return type checking
- Pointer type validation
- Test: type errors detected correctly

### Step 3.4: Storage layout
- Compute record sizes and field offsets from level declarations
- Compute array sizes
- Assign stack offsets for AUTOMATIC variables
- Assign static addresses for STATIC variables
- Test: layout matches expected sizes

---

## Phase 4: Code Generation Basics

### Step 4.1: Assembly emitter framework
- Output buffer and emit functions
- Section management (.text, .data)
- Label generation (unique labels for branches)
- Comment emission (source line tracking)
- Test: emit skeleton .s file

### Step 4.2: Expression codegen
- Literal loading (mvi)
- Variable load/store (lw/sw with offsets)
- Binary arithmetic (add, sub, mul)
- Software divide routine
- Comparison to branch conditions
- Register allocation for expression temporaries
- Test: evaluate expressions, print results via UART

### Step 4.3: Assignment codegen
- Simple variable assignment
- Record field assignment (base + offset)
- Array element assignment (base + index * size)
- Test: assign and read back values

### Step 4.4: Static data emission
- String literals in .data section
- Static variable allocation
- Initial values
- Test: static data accessible at runtime

---

## Phase 5: Procedures and Calling Convention

### Step 5.1: Procedure prologue/epilogue
- Push fp, save callee-saved registers
- Set up frame pointer
- Allocate stack space for locals
- Epilogue: restore and return
- Test: empty procedure call/return works

### Step 5.2: Procedure calls
- Evaluate arguments, push right-to-left
- CALL instruction
- Clean up stack after return
- Capture return value from r0
- Test: call with arguments, get return value

### Step 5.3: Nested calls and recursion
- Correct frame management for nested calls
- Register save/restore across calls
- Test: recursive factorial

---

## Phase 6: Control Flow

### Step 6.1: IF/THEN/ELSE
- Evaluate condition
- Conditional branch to else/end label
- Test: if/then, if/then/else

### Step 6.2: DO WHILE loop
- Loop header label
- Condition evaluation and branch
- Loop body
- Branch back to header
- Test: counted loop via while

### Step 6.3: DO count loop (iteration)
- Loop variable, start, end, optional increment
- Lowered to while-equivalent
- Test: DO I = 1 TO 10

---

## Phase 7: Records and Arrays

### Step 7.1: Record field access
- Dot notation: `rec.field`
- Computed offset from record layout
- Nested record access: `rec.sub.field`
- Test: declare, assign, read record fields

### Step 7.2: Array access
- Index expression: `arr(i)`
- Bounds checking (optional, controlled by option)
- Multi-dimensional arrays (future)
- Test: declare, fill, read array

---

## Phase 8: Pointers and Addresses

### Step 8.1: Pointer operations
- ADDR(var) -- address-of operator
- PTR-> dereference
- Pointer arithmetic (add offset)
- Test: pointer to record, pointer to array element

### Step 8.2: BASED storage (future)
- Variables accessed via explicit base pointer
- Overlay semantics
- Test: control block access via pointer

---

## Phase 9: Inline Assembly

### Step 9.1: ASM DO blocks
- Parse ASM DO; "string"; ... END;
- Emit assembly strings verbatim into .s output
- String interpolation for variable references
- Test: inline asm that manipulates registers

### Step 9.2: Naked procedures
- PROC with OPTIONS(NAKED) skips prologue/epilogue
- Entire body is ASM
- Test: interrupt handler stub

---

## Phase 10: Macro Preprocessor

### Step 10.1: Include processing
- %INCLUDE directive
- Search path from -I flags
- Read and insert .msw file contents
- Test: include a .msw file

### Step 10.2: Macro definition parsing
- Parse MACRODEF ... END; in .msw files
- Store macro name, clause grammar, body
- REQUIRED and OPTIONAL clauses
- Test: parse a macro definition

### Step 10.3: Macro expansion
- Recognize ?MACRO(...) in source
- Match keyword arguments to clauses
- Substitute arguments into macro body
- Expand to PL/SW source tokens
- Test: ?GETMAIN expands correctly

### Step 10.4: GEN blocks
- GEN DO; ... END; within macro bodies
- Emit assembly directly during expansion
- Template substitution for arguments
- Test: macro with GEN emits correct assembly

### Step 10.5: Conditional compilation
- %IF / %THEN / %ELSE / %ENDIF
- Based on defined symbols or target flags
- Test: conditional include/exclude

---

## Phase 11: Demo Programs and Testing

### Step 11.1: UART hello world
- PL/SW program that prints "Hello from PL/SW!" via UART
- End-to-end: .plsw -> compiler -> .s -> cor24-run

### Step 11.2: LED toggle
- MMIO write to LED address using inline ASM
- Test on emulator

### Step 11.3: Counted loop with output
- Loop printing numbers 1..10
- Demonstrates DO, CALL, arithmetic

### Step 11.4: Record and pointer demo
- Declare a record, fill fields, access via pointer
- Demonstrates level-based DCL, PTR, field access

### Step 11.5: Macro demo
- .msw file with service macro
- .plsw file invoking macro
- Demonstrates macro pipeline

---

## Phase 12: Polish and Documentation

### Step 12.1: Mixed listing output
- Source line + generated assembly interleaved
- Controlled by compiler flag

### Step 12.2: Error messages
- Line numbers in all error messages
- Descriptive error categories

### Step 12.3: Final documentation
- Update README with usage, examples
- Document language syntax reference
- Document macro system reference
- Example session transcripts

Changes summary:
- **src/lexer.h** — Added `%DEFINE` value substitution in identifier scanning: when an identifier matches a define with a non-empty value, substitute as `TOK_NUM` (numeric) or re-lookup as keyword/ident (text)
- **src/main.c** — Added 4 new tests (10-13) for value substitution: numeric, keyword, large number, flag-only (no substitution)
- **examples/define.plsw** — New example demonstrating `%DEFINE` compile-time constants
- **docs/usage.md** — Added value substitution documentation
- **README.md** — Updated feature line
- **.agentrail/** — Saga state updates (steps 41-43 added)

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(plsw): add %DEFINE value substitution in expressions

%DEFINE NAME VALUE now substitutes VALUE when NAME appears as an
identifier in expressions, enabling zero-cost compile-time constants.
Numeric values produce TOK_NUM tokens; text values are re-looked up
as keywords or identifiers. Flag-only defines (no value) are unchanged.

Adds 4 lexer tests, a define.plsw example, and usage docs.

Closes #1

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# PL/SW Compiler -- Implementation Plan

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Description                     | Deliverable                              |
|-------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 0     | Project setup and toolchain     | Build system, hello-world on emulator    |
| 1     | Lexer and token infrastructure  | Tokenize PL/SW source                   |
| 2     | Parser and AST                  | Parse core language constructs           |
| 3     | Symbol table and type system    | Scoped symbols, type checking            |
| 4     | Code generation basics          | Emit .s for simple programs              |
| 5     | Procedures and calling conv.    | PROC, CALL, RETURN with stack frames     |
| 6     | Control flow                    | IF/THEN/ELSE, DO WHILE, DO count        |
| 7     | Records and arrays              | Level-based structures, array access     |
| 8     | Pointers and addresses          | PTR type, address-of, dereference        |
| 9     | Inline assembly                 | ASM DO blocks, naked procedures          |
| 10    | Macro preprocessor              | .msw parsing, ?MACRO expansion, GEN      |
| 11    | Demo programs and testing       | UART hello, LED toggle, service wrappers |
| 12    | Polish and documentation        | Listings, error messages, docs           |

---

## Phase 0: Project Setup and Toolchain Validation

### Step 0.1: Repository structure
- Create `src/` for compiler C source
- Create `test/` for test PL/SW programs
- Create `examples/` for demo programs
- Create build script for tc24r + cor24-run pipeline
- Verify tc24r compiles a trivial C program
- Verify cor24-run assembles and executes it

### Step 0.2: UART I/O bootstrap
- Write uart_putchar, uart_getchar, uart_puts, uart_getline
- Write print_int for decimal output
- Test: compiler shell prints banner and reads input

### Step 0.3: String and memory utilities
- Write strcmp, strcpy, strlen, memset, memcpy
- Write simple arena allocator for compiler tables
- Test: basic string operations work

---

## Phase 1: Lexer and Token Infrastructure

### Step 1.1: Token types and keyword table
- Define token types: DCL, PROC, IF, THEN, ELSE, DO, WHILE, END,
  CALL, RETURN, ASM, IDENT, NUM, operators, punctuation
- Keyword lookup table (DCL, DECLARE, PROC, IF, THEN, ...)
- Test: identify keywords vs identifiers

### Step 1.2: Lexer implementation
- Scan input buffer character by character
- Handle: identifiers, numbers, string literals, operators
- Skip whitespace and comments (/* ... */ style)
- Handle multi-character operators (<=, >=, !=, ->)
- Test: tokenize sample PL/SW fragments

---

## Phase 2: Parser and AST

### Step 2.1: AST node pool and types
- Pre-allocated node pool (256 nodes)
- Node kinds: PROGRAM, DCL, PROC, IF, DO_WHILE, ASSIGN,
  BINOP, UNOP, CALL, RETURN, LITERAL, IDENT, ASM_BLOCK
- Allocator and tree-building helpers

### Step 2.2: Declaration parser
- Parse DCL statements with type attributes
- Parse level-based structure declarations
- Parse storage class attributes (STATIC, AUTOMATIC, EXTERNAL)
- Test: parse various DCL forms

### Step 2.3: Expression parser
- Binary operators: +, -, *, arithmetic
- Comparison operators: =, <, >, <=, >=, !=
- Logical operators: &, |, ^, ~ (bitwise)
- Shift operators: << >> (or SHL, SHR keywords)
- Parenthesized sub-expressions
- Operator precedence (standard arithmetic precedence)
- Test: parse expression trees

### Step 2.4: Statement parser
- Assignment: `var = expr;`
- IF/THEN/ELSE
- DO WHILE, DO count
- CALL proc(args)
- RETURN(expr)
- DO; ... END; blocks
- Test: parse compound statements

### Step 2.5: Procedure parser
- PROC name OPTIONS(...); ... END;
- Parameter declarations
- Local declarations
- RETURNS(type) clause
- Test: parse complete procedures

### Step 2.6: Top-level parser
- Parse sequence of top-level DCL and PROC
- Build program AST
- Test: parse multi-procedure programs

---

## Phase 3: Symbol Table and Type System

### Step 3.1: Symbol table
- Scoped symbol table (global + per-procedure)
- Insert, lookup, scope enter/exit
- Track: name, type, storage class, offset, level
- Test: nested scopes resolve correctly

### Step 3.2: Type representation
- Internal type tags: INT8, INT16, INT24, BYTE, CHAR, PTR,
  ARRAY, RECORD
- Width and signedness attributes
- Record field lists with offsets
- Test: type construction and comparison

### Step 3.3: Type checking
- Expression type inference and checking
- Assignment compatibility
- Procedure argument/return type checking
- Pointer type validation
- Test: type errors detected correctly

### Step 3.4: Storage layout
- Compute record sizes and field offsets from level declarations
- Compute array sizes
- Assign stack offsets for AUTOMATIC variables
- Assign static addresses for STATIC variables
- Test: layout matches expected sizes

---

## Phase 4: Code Generation Basics

### Step 4.1: Assembly emitter framework
- Output buffer and emit functions
- Section management (.text, .data)
- Label generation (unique labels for branches)
- Comment emission (source line tracking)
- Test: emit skeleton .s file

### Step 4.2: Expression codegen
- Literal loading (mvi)
- Variable load/store (lw/sw with offsets)
- Binary arithmetic (add, sub, mul)
- Software divide routine
- Comparison to branch conditions
- Register allocation for expression temporaries
- Test: evaluate expressions, print results via UART

### Step 4.3: Assignment codegen
- Simple variable assignment
- Record field assignment (base + offset)
- Array element assignment (base + index * size)
- Test: assign and read back values

### Step 4.4: Static data emission
- String literals in .data section
- Static variable allocation
- Initial values
- Test: static data accessible at runtime

---

## Phase 5: Procedures and Calling Convention

### Step 5.1: Procedure prologue/epilogue
- Push fp, save callee-saved registers
- Set up frame pointer
- Allocate stack space for locals
- Epilogue: restore and return
- Test: empty procedure call/return works

### Step 5.2: Procedure calls
- Evaluate arguments, push right-to-left
- CALL instruction
- Clean up stack after return
- Capture return value from r0
- Test: call with arguments, get return value

### Step 5.3: Nested calls and recursion
- Correct frame management for nested calls
- Register save/restore across calls
- Test: recursive factorial

---

## Phase 6: Control Flow

### Step 6.1: IF/THEN/ELSE
- Evaluate condition
- Conditional branch to else/end label
- Test: if/then, if/then/else

### Step 6.2: DO WHILE loop
- Loop header label
- Condition evaluation and branch
- Loop body
- Branch back to header
- Test: counted loop via while

### Step 6.3: DO count loop (iteration)
- Loop variable, start, end, optional increment
- Lowered to while-equivalent
- Test: DO I = 1 TO 10

---

## Phase 7: Records and Arrays

### Step 7.1: Record field access
- Dot notation: `rec.field`
- Computed offset from record layout
- Nested record access: `rec.sub.field`
- Test: declare, assign, read record fields

### Step 7.2: Array access
- Index expression: `arr(i)`
- Bounds checking (optional, controlled by option)
- Multi-dimensional arrays (future)
- Test: declare, fill, read array

---

## Phase 8: Pointers and Addresses

### Step 8.1: Pointer operations
- ADDR(var) -- address-of operator
- PTR-> dereference
- Pointer arithmetic (add offset)
- Test: pointer to record, pointer to array element

### Step 8.2: BASED storage (future)
- Variables accessed via explicit base pointer
- Overlay semantics
- Test: control block access via pointer

---

## Phase 9: Inline Assembly

### Step 9.1: ASM DO blocks
- Parse ASM DO; "string"; ... END;
- Emit assembly strings verbatim into .s output
- String interpolation for variable references
- Test: inline asm that manipulates registers

### Step 9.2: Naked procedures
- PROC with OPTIONS(NAKED) skips prologue/epilogue
- Entire body is ASM
- Test: interrupt handler stub

---

## Phase 10: Macro Preprocessor

### Step 10.1: Include processing
- %INCLUDE directive
- Search path from -I flags
- Read and insert .msw file contents
- Test: include a .msw file

### Step 10.2: Macro definition parsing
- Parse MACRODEF ... END; in .msw files
- Store macro name, clause grammar, body
- REQUIRED and OPTIONAL clauses
- Test: parse a macro definition

### Step 10.3: Macro expansion
- Recognize ?MACRO(...) in source
- Match keyword arguments to clauses
- Substitute arguments into macro body
- Expand to PL/SW source tokens
- Test: ?GETMAIN expands correctly

### Step 10.4: GEN blocks
- GEN DO; ... END; within macro bodies
- Emit assembly directly during expansion
- Template substitution for arguments
- Test: macro with GEN emits correct assembly

### Step 10.5: Conditional compilation
- %IF / %THEN / %ELSE / %ENDIF
- Based on defined symbols or target flags
- Test: conditional include/exclude

---

## Phase 11: Demo Programs and Testing

### Step 11.1: UART hello world
- PL/SW program that prints "Hello from PL/SW!" via UART
- End-to-end: .plsw -> compiler -> .s -> cor24-run

### Step 11.2: LED toggle
- MMIO write to LED address using inline ASM
- Test on emulator

### Step 11.3: Counted loop with output
- Loop printing numbers 1..10
- Demonstrates DO, CALL, arithmetic

### Step 11.4: Record and pointer demo
- Declare a record, fill fields, access via pointer
- Demonstrates level-based DCL, PTR, field access

### Step 11.5: Macro demo
- .msw file with service macro
- .plsw file invoking macro
- Demonstrates macro pipeline

---

## Phase 12: Polish and Documentation

### Step 12.1: Mixed listing output
- Source line + generated assembly interleaved
- Controlled by compiler flag

### Step 12.2: Error messages
- Line numbers in all error messages
- Descriptive error categories

### Step 12.3: Final documentation
- Update README with usage, examples
- Document language syntax reference
- Document macro system reference
- Example session transcripts

=== STEPS ===

=== PROMPT ===
Investigate and fix MACRODEF and DCL coexistence in .msw include files (GitHub issue #2). Currently, including an .msw file that has both DCL statements and MACRODEF blocks causes 'SYNTAX ERROR: unexpected top-level token' at the first MACRODEF after DCL. This forces splitting every module into two files (one for declarations, one for macros), which doubles include files and wastes include buffer space. Investigate the top-level parser's include processing path to understand why MACRODEF is not accepted after DCL during include expansion. The fix is likely allowing the parser to check for MACRODEF in the same place it checks for DCL at top level during include processing. Implement the fix (Option A preferred), add a test case with mixed DCL+MACRODEF in one .msw file, and verify existing examples still work.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

## Phase Overview

|-------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 0     | Project setup and toolchain     | Build system, hello-world on emulator    |
| 1     | Lexer and token infrastructure  | Tokenize PL/SW source                   |

| 3     | Symbol table and type system    | Scoped symbols, type checking            |

## Phase 1: Lexer and Token Infrastructure

### Step 1.2: Lexer implementation

=== PROMPT ===
Investigate and fix MACRODEF and DCL coexistence in .msw include files (GitHub issue #2). Currently, including an .msw file that has both DCL statements and MACRODEF blocks causes 'SYNTAX ERROR: unexpected top-level token' at the first MACRODEF after DCL. This forces splitting every module into two files (one for declarations, one for macros), which doubles include files and wastes include buffer space. Investigate the top-level parser's include processing path to understand why MACRODEF is not accepted after DCL during include expansion. The fix is likely allowing the parser to check for MACRODEF in the same place it checks for DCL at top level during include processing. Implement the fix (Option A preferred), add a test case with mixed DCL+MACRODEF in one .msw file, and verify existing examples still work.

Now let me debug the actual problem. Let me add some diagnostic output to `parse_program` to see what token it's getting.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The include content is:
```
DCL MVAR INT(24); MACRODEF MTEST; REQUIRED PTR(expr); GEN DO; 'mvi r0,0'; END; END;
```

After `parse_dcl()` processes `DCL MVAR INT(24);`, the parser has consumed through the semicolon. `parse_advance()` was last called in `parse_expect(TOK_SEMI)`. After consuming the semi, `parse_advance` calls `lex_scan()`, which reads the next token.

Let me check `parse_advance` and `parse_expect`.

So `parse_expect(TOK_SEMI)` at line 42-53: it checks if current token is TOK_SEMI, then calls `parse_advance()` (which calls `lex_scan()`). After that, `cur_type` has the next token — which should be `MACRODEF` (TOK_IDENT).

Let me verify by adding temporary debug output to `parse_program`.

Hmm wait. I just realized: `MACRODEF` is hitting the `TOK_IDENT` branch at line 833. But let me check — could `MACRODEF` be getting intercepted by the `%DEFINE` substitution I just added? No, there's no define for MACRODEF.

content.text:
So the first pass through, `MACRODEF` IS seen as `TOK_IDENT`. `mac_parse_def()` is called. But then `mac_parse_def()` must be failing or leaving the parser in a bad state. After `mac_parse_def()` returns, the loop `continue`s, and the NEXT iteration sees `PTR` (type 19) — which is not DCL, PROC, or IDENT. That's the "unexpected top-level token" error!

So `mac_parse_def()` is NOT fully consuming the macro definition. Let me check what `mac_parse_def` does with `REQUIRED PTR(expr)`. In this case, `PTR` is a keyword token (TOK_PTR = 19), not TOK_IDENT. But `mac_parse_def` expects `cur_type == TOK_IDENT` for clause parsing.

Let me look at `mac_parse_clause`.

Now I see — the clause parsing loop at lines 263-271 checks for `REQUIRED` or `OPTIONAL` as `TOK_IDENT`. Since `REQUIRED` and `OPTIONAL` are not keywords, they come through as `TOK_IDENT`. But what about the clause NAME? Let me check `mac_parse_clause`.

I see the issue! At line 136, `mac_parse_clause` expects `cur_type != TOK_IDENT` for the clause name. In my test, the clause is `REQUIRED PTR(expr)`. After consuming `REQUIRED`, `lex_scan()` returns the next token. But `PTR` is a keyword — `TOK_PTR` (19), not `TOK_IDENT` (40). So line 136 fails: `cur_type != TOK_IDENT` → `mac_error("expected clause name")`.

Wait no — `mac_error` sets `mac_parse_err` to 1 and returns 0. Then `mac_parse_def` at line 267 gets 0 from `mac_parse_clause`, does `break`, exits the clause loop. Then at line 273, `mac_parse_err` is true, so it returns -1.

But then `parse_program` at line 836 called `mac_parse_def()` which returned -1. After `mac_parse_def()` returns, `parse_program` does `continue` (line 837). But wait — `mac_parse_def` returned -1 (error), and the tokens were NOT fully consumed. The lexer is sitting somewhere in the middle of the macro definition.

Actually, let me re-check. The test content is:
```
REQUIRED PTR(expr)
```

After `REQUIRED` is consumed at line 128, `lex_scan()` gives `PTR` which is `TOK_PTR` (19). Line 136 checks `cur_type != TOK_IDENT` — this is TRUE (it's TOK_PTR, not TOK_IDENT). So `mac_error("expected clause name")` is called and returns 0.

This is the real bug — `mac_parse_clause` doesn't accept keyword tokens as clause names. The fix should be in `mac_parse_clause` to accept any identifier-like token (keywords are also valid clause names since they're just naming the macro parameter).

Actually wait, the test I wrote uses `REQUIRED PTR(expr)` — `PTR` is indeed a keyword in PL/SW. But in a MACRODEF clause, `PTR` is just the clause parameter name. The user's real include file uses something like `REQUIRED PTR(expr)` where PTR is a parameter name.

Let me look at what the issue actually uses. Looking back at the issue:

```msw
MACRODEF BUILD_NULL;
    REQUIRED PTR(expr);
    GEN DO; 'la r2,{PTR}'; ... END;
END;
```

So `PTR` is indeed the clause name. The fix is to allow keyword tokens as clause names in `mac_parse_clause`. Let me also check the SNOBOL4 project.

Actually, the problem is twofold:
1. `mac_parse_clause` rejects keyword tokens as clause names
2. `parse_program` doesn't check `mac_parse_def`'s return value, so it continues even on error with partially-consumed tokens

Let me first remove the debug output, then fix both issues.

Also, I notice that `descr.msw` uses `DCL TAG_NULL INT STATIC INIT(0)` — those tag constants are exactly the use case for `%DEFINE` value substitution from issue #1. With that fix, these could become `%DEFINE TAG_NULL 0;` with zero runtime cost.

# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.

content.text:
Changes:
- **src/macro.h** — Fix `mac_parse_clause` to accept keyword tokens (e.g. `PTR`, `INT`) as clause names, not just `TOK_IDENT`
- **src/parser.h** — Propagate `mac_parse_def` errors in `parse_program` instead of silently continuing with partially-consumed tokens
- **src/main.c** — Add test case for mixed DCL+MACRODEF in a single .msw include file
- **.agentrail/** — Saga state updates

content.text: ## Phase 4 — Commit

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(plsw): allow MACRODEF and DCL to coexist in .msw includes

The root cause was mac_parse_clause rejecting keyword tokens (PTR,
INT, etc.) as clause names -- it only accepted TOK_IDENT. When a
MACRODEF like `REQUIRED PTR(expr)` was encountered, `PTR` lexed as
TOK_PTR (a keyword), causing mac_parse_def to fail silently. The
parser then continued with partially-consumed tokens, hitting
"unexpected top-level token" on whatever was left.

Two fixes:
1. mac_parse_clause now accepts any token with text as a clause name
2. parse_program propagates mac_parse_def errors instead of ignoring

Adds test for mixed DCL+MACRODEF in one include file.

Closes #2

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Step 42 done. The bug was that `mac_parse_clause` only accepted `TOK_IDENT` as clause names, but PL/SW keywords like `PTR` lex as their own token types. When a MACRODEF used `REQUIRED PTR(expr)`, the parser choked because `PTR` wasn't `TOK_IDENT`.

The SNOBOL4 project at `sw-cor24-snobol4` confirms the impact — it had to split `descr.msw` into two files with a comment documenting the limitation.

Next session: `agentrail next` for step 043 (increase include buffer).
